name: Pyramid Power Effects
description: Pyramid power is a pseudoscientific belief that the Great Pyramid of Giza and pyramids of similar proportions possess supernatural properties capable of preserving food, sharpening blades, improving health, and even influencing mental states. The theory gained traction in the mid-20th century, popularized by authors like Patrick Flanagan and Karel Drbal, who claimed to have experimentally verified these effects. Proponents often attribute these powers to the pyramid's shape acting as a resonator or focusing device for cosmic energy, unknown forces, or even the Earth's magnetic field. Evidence cited includes anecdotal accounts of food preservation, blade sharpening, and altered plant growth within pyramid models. Mainstream science dismisses pyramid power as lacking empirical support and attributing any observed effects to placebo, confirmation bias, or variations in environmental conditions. Controlled experiments consistently fail to replicate the claimed results. Skeptics point out the lack of a scientifically plausible mechanism for such effects and highlight the selective reporting and methodological flaws in studies supporting pyramid power. The connection to ancient Egyptian beliefs is tenuous, as there is no historical evidence suggesting that the ancient Egyptians attributed any special powers to the shape of pyramids beyond their function as tombs and symbols of royal power. The modern interpretation of 'pyramid power' is largely a 20th-century invention, distinct from ancient Egyptian religious or architectural practices.
subtitle: Pseudoscientific belief in pyramid's special powers.

claims.evidence: Anecdotal accounts of food placed inside pyramid models showing slower decay.
claims.criticism: Lack of controlled experiments; potential for placebo effect or environmental factors influencing decay rates.

claims.evidence: Claims that dull razor blades regain sharpness when placed inside a pyramid.
claims.criticism: No scientific evidence supporting this; potential for microscopic corrosion or other factors to temporarily alter blade's edge.

claims.claim: Pyramids enhance meditation and mental clarity.
claims.evidence: Subjective reports of improved focus and relaxation while meditating inside or near pyramids.
claims.criticism: Placebo effect; lack of objective measurement; psychological effects of novelty or suggestion.

keyFigures.name: Patrick Flanagan
keyFigures.role: Author and proponent of pyramid power.

keyFigures.name: Karel Drbal
keyFigures.role: Czech inventor who patented a 'razor blade sharpener' based on pyramid shape.

keyFigures.name: Martin Gardner
keyFigures.role: Science writer and critic who debunked many pyramid power claims.

timeline.event: Popularization of pyramid power theories through books and articles.
Ongoing research (mostly pseudoscientific) and debate about pyramid power effects.

sources.author: Martin Gardner
sources.work: Fads and Fallacies in the Name of Science
sources.title: Martin Gardner
